content:
# Numbers Chapter 27 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 181-182 | **Source Image:** `NUM_027_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_027_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. pa_0134-pa_0135

---

**1** And the daughters of Zelophehad stepped forward — Zelophehad son of Hepher, son of Gilead, son of Machir, son of Manasseh, from the clans of Manasseh son of Joseph. These were the names of his daughters: Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah.

**2** And they stood before Moses and before Eleazar the priest and before the leaders and the entire community, at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting, and said:

**3** "Our father died in the wilderness. He was not among the faction that banded together against YHWH — the faction of Korah — but he died for his own sin, and he had no sons.

**4** Why should our father's name disappear from his clan just because he had no son? Give us a land holding among our father's kinsmen."

**5** And Moses brought their case before YHWH.

**6** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying:

**7** "The daughters of Zelophehad are right in what they say. You must certainly give them a hereditary land holding among their father's kinsmen, and transfer their father's inheritance to them.

**8** And speak to the Sons of Israel, saying: 'When a man dies without leaving a son, you shall transfer his inheritance to his daughter.

**9** And if he has no daughter, you shall give his inheritance to his brothers.

**10** And if he has no brothers, you shall give his inheritance to his father's brothers.

**11** And if his father has no brothers, you shall give his inheritance to his nearest relative within his clan, and that person shall take possession of it.' This shall be a binding rule of law for the Sons of Israel, just as YHWH commanded Moses."

**12** And YHWH said to Moses, "Go up to this mountain of the Abarim range and look out over the land that I have given to the Sons of Israel.

**13** And when you have seen it, you too will be gathered to your people, just as your brother Aaron was gathered —

**14** because you both rebelled against my command in the wilderness of Zin, when the community quarreled, and you failed to uphold my holiness before their eyes at the waters." (These are the waters of Meribah-kadesh, in the wilderness of Zin.)

**15** And Moses spoke to YHWH, saying:

**16** "Let YHWH, the God of the spirits of all living beings, appoint someone over the community

**17** who will go out before them and come in before them, who will lead them out and bring them in — so that the community of YHWH will not be like sheep without a shepherd."

**18** And YHWH said to Moses, "Take Joshua son of Nun, a man in whom the spirit dwells, and lay your hand upon him.

**19** And stand him before Eleazar the priest and before the entire community, and commission him in their sight.

**20** And invest him with some of your authority, so that the entire community of the Sons of Israel will obey him.

**21** And he shall stand before Eleazar the priest, who will seek guidance for him through the judgment of the Urim before YHWH. At his word they shall go out, and at his word they shall come in — he and all the Sons of Israel with him, the entire community."

**22** And Moses did just as YHWH commanded him. He took Joshua and stood him before Eleazar the priest and before the entire community.

**23** And he laid his hands upon him and commissioned him, just as YHWH had spoken through Moses.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "stepped forward":** The Hebrew ותקרבנה (*watiqravnah*) means literally "drew near" or "approached." I render it as "stepped forward" to convey the deliberate boldness of these women presenting themselves before the assembled leadership — this was not a casual request but a courageous public act.

**Verse 3 — "he died for his own sin":** The Hebrew בחטאו מת (*b'chet'o met*) is a compressed phrase. The daughters are making a legal distinction: their father was not part of the Korah rebellion (which would have carried the stigma of communal punishment), but rather died as all the wilderness generation died — under the general decree of Numbers 14. This is a legal argument, not a confession of particular wickedness.

**Verse 4 — "Why should our father's name disappear":** The Hebrew למה יגרע (*lammah yiggara'*) means literally "why should it be diminished/withdrawn." The daughters frame this as an issue of justice: without land, a family name is effectively erased from Israel's memory. The dynamic rendering "disappear" captures the existential stakes they felt.

**Verse 7 — "are right in what they say":** The Hebrew כן בנות צלפחד דברת (*ken b'not Tz'lofchad dov'rot*) is literally "right/correct the daughters of Zelophehad speak." YHWH's response is direct and unequivocal — a remarkable divine vindication of women asserting legal rights in the ancient world.

**Verse 14 — "you failed to uphold my holiness":** The Hebrew להקדישני (*l'haqdisheni*) means "to sanctify me" or "to treat me as holy." The dynamic rendering "uphold my holiness" captures the relational weight: God entrusted Moses with representing divine authority, and at Meribah, Moses acted in a way that obscured rather than displayed God's character.

**Verse 16 — "God of the spirits of all living beings":** The Hebrew אלהי הרוחת לכל בשר (*Elohei harukhot l'khol basar*) is literally "God of the spirits of all flesh." This extraordinary title appears only here and in Numbers 16:22. Moses appeals to God as the one who knows the inner life of every person — the perfect qualifier for choosing the right leader.

**Verse 17 — "sheep without a shepherd":** This vivid image (צאן אשר אין להם רעה, *tson asher ein lahem ro'eh*) becomes one of the Bible's most enduring metaphors for leaderless, vulnerable people. Jesus echoes it in Matthew 9:36. Moses' concern is deeply pastoral — he does not want to leave the people exposed.

**Verse 20 — "invest him with some of your authority":** The Hebrew הודך (*hod'kha*) means "your splendor" or "your majesty." This is not mere bureaucratic delegation. Moses carried a visible dignity — a gravitas that made people listen. YHWH instructs Moses to share some of that presence with Joshua, though notably only "some" (מ, *min*), not all.

**Verse 21 — "the judgment of the Urim":** The Urim (אורים, *urim*) was a sacred object kept within the high priest's breastpiece, used to discern divine will. Where Moses spoke with God face to face, Joshua's access to divine guidance would be mediated through the priestly oracle — marking a shift in how Israel's leadership would function going forward.

-- PNEUMA
